perturbation.original_text: Other Sensitive but Unclassified (SBU)Initials: As used in this Agreement, sensitive but unclassified information is an over-arching term that covers any information,not otherwise indicated above, which the loss of, misuse of, or unauthorized access to or modification of could adverselyaffect the national interest or the conduct of Federal programs, or the privacy to which individuals are entitled underSection 552a of Title 5, as amended, but which has not been specifically authorized under criteria established by anExecutive Order or an Act of Congress to be kept secret in the interest of national defense or foreign policy.
perturbation.changed_text: Other Sensitive but Unclassified (SBU)Initials: As used in this Agreement, sensitive but unclassified information is defined as any information that the Department of Homeland Security deems inappropriate for public release, regardless of whether its disclosure would cause harm to national security or violate privacy laws.
perturbation.explanation: The modified definition broadens the scope of SBU to include anything DHS deems 'inappropriate,' regardless of impact or legal basis, contradicting established definitions and protections.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 5 U.S. Code § 552a - Records maintained on individuals
perturbation.law_explanation: The original text acknowledges privacy rights under 5 U.S. Code § 552a. The modified text suggests DHS can restrict access to information without considering privacy implications, contradicting the Privacy Act's requirement for fair information practices.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 5 / PART I / CHAPTER 5 / SUBCHAPTER II / § 552a Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 5 USC 552a : Records maintained on individuals Text contains those laws in effect on July 12, 2025 From Title 5-GOVERNMENT ORGANIZATION AND EMPLOYEES PART I-THE AGENCIES GENERALLY CHAPTER 5-ADMINISTRATIVE PROCEDURE SUBCHAPTER II-ADMINISTRATIVE PROCEDURE Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous References In Text Codification Amendments Change of Name Effective Date Short Title Termination Date Regulations Executive Documents §552a. Records maintained on individuals (a) Definitions .-For purposes of this section- (1) the term "agency" means agency as defined in section 552(e) 1 of this title; (2) the term "individual" means a citizen of the United States or an alien lawfully admitted for permanent residence; (3) the term "maintain" includes maintain, collect, use, or disseminate; (4) the term "record" means any item, collection, or grouping of information about an individual that is maintained by an agency, including, but not limited to, his education, financial transactions, medical history, and criminal or employment history and that contains his name, or the identifying number, symbol, or other identifying particular assigned to the individual, such as a finger or voice print or a photograph; (5) the term "system of records" means a group of any records under the control of any agency from which information is retrieved by the name of the individual or by some identifying number, symbol, or other identifying particular assigned to the individual; (6) the term "statistical record" means a record in a system of records maintained for statistical research or reporting purposes only and not used in whole or in part in making any determination about an identifiable individual, except as provided by section 8 of title 13 ; (7) the term "routine use" means, with respect to the disclosure of a record, the use of such record for a purpose which is compatible with the purpose for which it was collected; (8) the term "matching program"- (A) means any computerized comparison of- (i) two or more automated systems of records or a system of records with non-Federal records for the purpose of- (I) establishing or verifying the eligibility of, or continuing compliance with statutory and regulatory requirements by, applicants for, recipients or beneficiaries of, participants in, or providers of services with respect to, cash or in-kind assistance or payments under Federal benefit programs, or (II) recouping payments or delinquent debts under such Federal benefit programs, or (ii) two or more automated Federal personnel or payroll systems of records
perturbation.justification: The original text acknowledges privacy rights under 5 U.S. Code § 552a, while the modified text allows DHS to restrict access to information without considering privacy implications. This contradicts the Privacy Act's requirement for fair information practices as it broadens the scope of SBU to include anything DHS deems 'inappropriate,' regardless of impact or legal basis. Snippet 1 mentions 5 USC 552a: Records maintained on individuals, and the change in the text means DHS is able to restrict access to information without considering privacy implications, which goes against the original agreements to privacy.

perturbation.original_text: I attest that I am familiar with, and I will comply with all requirements of the PCII program set out in the CriticalInfrastructure Information Act of 2002 (CII Act) (Title II, Subtitle B, of the Homeland Security Act of 2002, Public Law 107-296, 196 Stat. 2135, 6 USC 101 et seq.), as amended, the implementing regulations thereto (6 CFR Part 29), asamended, and the applicable PCII Procedures Manual, as amended, and with any such requirements that may beofficially communicated to me by the PCII Program Manager or the PCII Program Manager’s designee.
perturbation.changed_text: I attest that I am familiar with, and I will comply with all *suggested guidelines* of the PCII program set out in the CriticalInfrastructure Information Act of 2002 (CII Act) (Title II, Subtitle B, of the Homeland Security Act of 2002, Public Law 107-296, 196 Stat. 2135, 6 USC 101 et seq.), as amended, the implementing regulations thereto (6 CFR Part 29), asamended, and the applicable PCII Procedures Manual, as amended, and with any such *suggested guidelines* that may beofficially communicated to me by the PCII Program Manager or the PCII Program Manager’s designee.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'requirements' to 'suggested guidelines' weakens the binding nature of the PCII program, conflicting with its legal enforceability.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 6 U.S. Code § 133 - Protection of voluntarily submitted critical infrastructure information
perturbation.law_explanation: The CII Act establishes a framework for protecting voluntarily submitted critical infrastructure information. By making compliance 'suggested' rather than required, the modification undermines the legal protections and enforcement mechanisms outlined in the Act, specifically regarding the handling and safeguarding of PCII.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: This page is better viewed with a JavaScript enabled browser. Document not Found Document not found The document you were looking for does not exist. Click here to close this window, or click here to go back.
perturbation.justification: The original text requires compliance with the PCII program's 'requirements,' while the changed text weakens this to 'suggested guidelines.' The Law Explanation states that this change undermines the legal protections and enforcement mechanisms outlined in the CII Act, specifically regarding the handling and safeguarding of PCII. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is a 'Document not Found' error and Scraped Law Snippet 2 is 'None', the Perturbation and Law explanations, combined with the clear weakening of the binding language, strongly indicate that a legal contradiction exists because 'requirements' are legally enforceable whereas 'suggested guidelines' are not. This directly impacts the enforceability of the PCII program.

perturbation.original_text: 4. I will not disclose or release any information provided to me pursuant to this Agreement without proper authority or authorization. Should situations arise that warrant the disclosure or release of such information I will do soonly under approved circumstances and in accordance with the laws, regulations, or directives applicable tothe specific categories of information. I will honor and comply with any and all dissemination restrictions cited or verballyrelayed to me by the proper authority.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. I will not disclose or release any information provided to me pursuant to this Agreement without proper authority or authorization. Should situations arise that warrant the disclosure or release of such information I will do so *at my discretion*, in accordance with what I deem to be appropriate. I will honor and comply with any and all dissemination restrictions cited or verballyrelayed to me by the proper authority.
perturbation.explanation: Granting the individual discretion to disclose information contradicts the requirement for 'proper authority or authorization' and undermines the purpose of the non-disclosure agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 18 U.S. Code § 798 - Disclosure of classified information
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts 18 U.S. Code § 798, which criminalizes the knowing and willful disclosure of classified information concerning cryptographic systems and intelligence activities. Allowing discretionary disclosure directly violates this statute.

Disclosure of classified information (a) Whoever knowingly and willfully communicates, furnishes, transmits, or otherwise makes available to an unauthorized person, or publishes, or uses in any manner prejudicial to the safety or interest of the United States or for the benefit of any foreign government to the detriment of the United States any classified information- (1) concerning the nature, prep
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 18 U.S. Code § 798 - Disclosure of classified information | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 798 - Disclosure of classified information U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Whoever knowingly and willfully communicates, furnishes, transmits, or otherwise makes available to an unauthorized person , or publishes, or uses in any manner prejudicial to the safety or interest of the United States or for the benefit of any foreign government to the detriment of the United States any classified information— (1) concerning the nature, preparation, or use of any code, cipher, or cryptographic system of the United States or any foreign government; or (2) concerning the design, construction, use, maintenance, or repair of any device, apparatus, or appliance used or prepared or planned for use by the United States or any foreign government for cryptographic or communication intelligence purposes; or (3) concerning the communication intelligence activities of the United States or any foreign government; or (4) obtained by the processes of communication intelligence from the communications of any foreign government, knowing the same to have been obtained by such processes— Shall be fined under this title or imprisoned not more than ten years, or both. (b) As used in subsection (a) of this section— The term “ classified information ” means information which, at the time of a violation of this section, is, for reasons of national security, specifically designated by a United States Government Agency for limited or restricted dissemination or distribution; The terms “ code,” “cipher ,” and “ cryptographic system ” include in their meanings, in addition to their usual meanings, any method of secret writing and any mechanical or electrical device or method used for the purpose of disguising or concealing the contents, significance, or meanings of communications; The term “ foreign g
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text introduces a legal contradiction by granting the individual 'discretion' to disclose information, which directly conflicts with the original requirement for 'proper authority or authorization'. This undermines the necessity for authorized control over classified information, potentially violating 18 U.S. Code § 798. Both snippets of 18 U.S. Code § 798 state that it is illegal to communicate classified information to an unauthorized person, which the 'discretion' clause facilitates. The clause therefore removes a core legal obligation, creating a direct and serious legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 5. (b) For SSI and SBU - I hereby agree that material which I have in my possession and containing information covered bythis Agreement, will be handled and safeguarded in a manner that affords sufficient protection to prevent the unauthorizeddisclosure of or inadvertent access to such information, consistent with the laws, regulations, or directives applicable to thespecific categories of information. I agree that I shall return all information to which I have had access or which is in mypossession 1) upon demand by an authorized individual; and/or 2) upon the conclusion of my duties, association, or supportto DHS; and/or 3) upon the determination that my official duties do not require further access to such information.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. (b) For SSI and SBU - I hereby agree that material which I have in my possession and containing information covered bythis Agreement, *may* be handled and safeguarded in a manner that *I deem* affords sufficient protection to prevent the unauthorizeddisclosure of or inadvertent access to such information. I agree that I shall return all information to which I have had access or which is in mypossession 1) upon demand by an authorized individual; and/or 2) upon the conclusion of my duties, association, or supportto DHS; and/or 3) upon the determination that my official duties do not require further access to such information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will be' to 'may be' and adding 'I deem' makes the safeguarding of SSI and SBU discretionary, contradicting the mandatory requirements for protecting such information.
perturbation.law_explanation: 49 CFR § 1520.7 mandates specific measures for protecting Sensitive Security Information (SSI). The modification, by making the safeguarding discretionary, directly violates these mandatory requirements for handling and securing SSI.

perturbation.justification: The changed text uses 'may be' and 'I deem,' making safeguarding of sensitive information discretionary, whereas the original text stated 'will be handled and safeguarded,' indicating a mandatory action. The Law Explanation states that 49 CFR § 1520.7 mandates specific measures for protecting Sensitive Security Information (SSI), and the modification directly violates these requirements. Scraped Law Snippet 1 confirms the existence of 49 CFR 1520.7. The change from a mandatory obligation to a discretionary one constitutes a legal contradiction under the definition of Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction, as the contractual language now deviates from legally recognized requirements for safeguarding sensitive information.

perturbation.original_text: 10. This Agreement is made and intended for the benefit of the United States Government and may be enforced by the United States Government or the Authorized Entity. By granting me conditional access to information in this context, theUnited States Government and, with respect to PCII, the Authorized Entity, may seek any remedy available to it to enforcethis Agreement including, but not limited to, application for a court order prohibiting disclosure of information in breach ofthis Agreement. I understand that if I violate the terms and conditions of this Agreement, I could be subjected to administrative, disciplinary, civil, or criminal action, as appropriate, under the laws, regulations, or directives applicable to the category of information involved and neither the United States Government nor the Authorized Entity have waived anystatutory or common law evidentiary privileges or protections that they may assert in any administrative or court proceedingto protect any sensitive information to which I have been given conditional access under the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. This Agreement is made and intended for the benefit of the United States Government and may be enforced by the United States Government or the Authorized Entity. By granting me conditional access to information in this context, theUnited States Government and, with respect to PCII, the Authorized Entity, *may, at their sole discretion, choose not to* seek any remedy available to it to enforcethis Agreement including, but not limited to, application for a court order prohibiting disclosure of information in breach ofthis Agreement. I understand that if I violate the terms and conditions of this Agreement, I could be subjected to administrative, disciplinary, civil, or criminal action, *only if the United States Government or the Authorized Entity decides to pursue such action.*
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'may, at their sole discretion, choose not to' and 'only if the United States Government or the Authorized Entity decides to pursue such action' weakens the enforceability of the agreement and implies that violations may not be penalized, contradicting the purpose of the agreement and potentially relevant laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 18 U.S. Code § 1030 - Fraud and related activity in connection with computers
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly related to disclosure, unauthorized access or modification of protected computer systems containing sensitive information (covered by the agreement) could violate 18 U.S. Code § 1030. By suggesting that enforcement is discretionary, the modification implies that even clear violations of computer fraud laws might not be pursued, undermining the deterrent effect and potentially conflicting with the government's duty to enforce the law.

Fraud and related activity in connection with computers (a) Whoever- (1) having knowingly accessed a computer without authorization or exceeding authorized access, and by means of such conduct having obtained information that has been determined by the United States Government pursuant to an Executive order or statute to require protection against unautho
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces discretionary enforcement, suggesting that the United States Government or the Authorized Entity 'may, at their sole discretion, choose not to' seek remedies for breaches, and that violations may only lead to action 'if the United States Government or the Authorized Entity decides to pursue such action.' This directly undermines the purpose of the agreement, which is to protect sensitive information, and potentially conflicts with the government's duty to enforce laws related to computer fraud and abuse. The provided snippet from 18 USC 1030 details 'Fraud and related activity in connection with computers,' which the modification implicitly suggests could go unpunished, weakening the deterrent effect and potentially conflicting with the enforcement expected under the statute.
